note_6_6A_0.js.title: Apparatus Calibration Often Overlooked
note_6_6A_0.js.text: In Unit 4/5 practical work, students frequently forget to calibrate instruments such as pH meters, thermometers, and gas syringes before use. Examiners specifically mark apparatus errors in method descriptions and error evaluation.

note_6_6A_0.js.title: Record Initial Observations Before Reaction
note_6_6A_0.js.text: Always note the initial colour, temperature, and physical state (solid/liquid/gas) of ALL reactants before mixing. Examiners expect detailed descriptions of the reaction sequence and final observations — omitting initial state is a common error costing marks.

note_6_6A_1.js.title: Control Variables Must Be Explicitly Named
note_6_6A_1.js.text: Students often list 'temperature', 'pressure' etc. without stating WHAT they are controlling them AT. Write 'temperature held constant at 25°C' not just 'controlled temperature' — examiners require specific numerical values or ranges for each variable controlled.

note_6_6A_1.js.title: Evidence of Preliminary Trial Work Earns Marks
note_6_6A_1.js.text: Demonstrating that you performed a preliminary investigation (e.g., 'I ran a trial with 2 cm³ to find optimal volume') scores highly with examiners. It shows scientific planning. Include rough data or brief notes on trials — omitting this suggests work was not thought through.

note_6_6A_2.js.title: Fe²⁺ Oxidation in Air — Colour Changes Over Time
note_6_6A_2.js.text: The green Fe²⁺ hydroxide precipitate [Fe(H₂O)₄(OH)₂] turns orange-brown within minutes as aerial oxidation converts Fe²⁺ to Fe³⁺. Examiners expect students to note the 'initial green → orange-brown' sequence as evidence of oxidation. Recording only the final colour loses marks.

note_6_6A_2.js.title: Precipitate Dissolution in Excess Ammonia Identifies Cu, Co, Ni — Memorise the Colours
note_6_6A_2.js.text: Cu²⁺ → deep blue [Cu(NH₃)₄]²⁺, Co²⁺ → yellow [Co(NH₃)₆]²⁺, Ni²⁺ → deep blue [Ni(NH₃)₆]²⁺. Students often confuse Cu and Ni (both deep blue) — examiners test this distinction. Write out the complex ion formula, not just 'blue'.

note_6_6A_3.js.title: Aldehyde/Ketone Test: Tollens vs Fehling — Use Correction Term
note_6_6A_3.js.text: Many students write 'the aldehyde tested positive with Tollens reagent' without stating the visual change: 'silver mirror formed' or 'a white precipitate formed'. Ketones show NO change with Tollens but DO with Fehling (orange-red ppt) — examiners mark down vague descriptions.

note_6_6A_3.js.title: Alcohols Primary vs Secondary: Oxidation Permanganate Colour Change is Diagnostic
note_6_6A_3.js.text: Primary alcohols undergo oxidation (acidified KMnO₄ turns colourless); tertiary alcohols do NOT (KMnO₄ stays purple). Examiners expect students to observe and record the colour change. Use this sequential oxidation: primary alcohol → aldehyde → carboxylic acid to explain why KMnO₄ is decolorised.

note_6_6A_4.js.title: Percentage Uncertainties Compound — Always State Absolute Uncertainties First
note_6_6A_4.js.text: Students frequently forget that percentage uncertainties of individual measurements are added (not averaged) to give total percentage uncertainty. Read the uncertainty from apparatus (burette ±0.05 cm³), calculate percentage, write it clearly: 'Total ±X%' before calculating absolute uncertainty in final answer.

note_6_6A_4.js.title: Rounding Significant Figures to Match Uncertainty
note_6_6A_4.js.text: If your uncertainty is ±0.02 (2 SF), your answer must be rounded to the same decimal place. Examiners penalise false precision: stating a result as '0.1234 mol' when uncertainty is ±0.15 mol suggests you did not understand error propagation.

note_6_6A_5.js.title: New Contexts Require Explicit Justification — Do Not Assume Knowledge Transfer
note_6_6A_5.js.text: When applying a technique to a new scenario, spell out the logical link. Do NOT write 'use the same method as for compound A'. Instead, write: 'Compound C is also an aldehyde, so testing with Tollens reagent is appropriate because [mechanism/reason].' Examiners expect evidence that you have reasoned, not just copied.

note_6_6A_5.js.title: Use Analogies to Justify Equipment and Procedure Changes in New Contexts
note_6_6A_5.js.text: If a practical is adapted (e.g., using a different starting material or temperature), explain why you chose the modified conditions by relating them to similar worked examples: 'Because X was insoluble in water, I heated the reaction mixture, similar to the preparation of Y in Core Practical Z.' This demonstrates critical thinking.
